title: Quickstart: Collaborative Analysis Workflow
text: Developer 2 adds new plotting function. Create R/plot_functions.R:
code: #' Create power-to-weight scatter plot #' #' @param data Processed mtcars data frame #' @param show_labels Logical, whether to show car labels #' @return A ggplot object #' @export plot_power_weight <- function(data, show_labels = TRUE) {   if (!"power_to_weight" %in% names(data)) {     stop("Data must contain power_to_weight column")   }    p <- ggplot2::ggplot(     data,     ggplot2::aes(x = weight_kg, y = hp, color = efficiency_class)   ) +     ggplot2::geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +     ggplot2::labs(       title = "Horsepower vs Weight by Efficiency Class",       x = "Weight (kg)",       y = "Horsepower",       color = "Efficiency"     ) +     ggplot2::theme_minimal() +     ggplot2::theme(legend.position = "bottom")    if (show_labels) {     p <- p + ggplot2::geom_text(       ggplot2::aes(label = rownames(data)),       hjust = -0.1,       vjust = 0.5,       size = 3     )   }    p }  #' Create cylinder comparison boxplot #' #' @param data Processed mtcars data frame #' @return A ggplot object #' @export plot_mpg_by_cylinder <- function(data) {   ggplot2::ggplot(data, ggplot2::aes(x = factor(cyl), y = mpg)) +     ggplot2::geom_boxplot(fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.7) +     ggplot2::geom_jitter(width = 0.2, alpha = 0.5) +     ggplot2::labs(       title = "Fuel Efficiency by Number of Cylinders",       x = "Number of Cylinders",       y = "Miles Per Gallon"     ) +     ggplot2::theme_minimal() }

title: Quickstart: Collaborative Analysis Workflow
text: Create tests/testthat/test-plot-functions.R:
code: # test-plot-functions.R # Tests for R/plot_functions.R  test_that("plot_power_weight returns ggplot object", {   test_data <- mtcars[1:5, ] |> add_derived_variables()    result <- plot_power_weight(test_data)    expect_s3_class(result, "ggplot") })  test_that("plot_power_weight requires power_to_weight column", {   bad_data <- mtcars[1:5, ]    expect_error(     plot_power_weight(bad_data),     "power_to_weight"   ) })  test_that("plot_power_weight respects show_labels parameter", {   test_data <- mtcars[1:5, ] |> add_derived_variables()    with_labels <- plot_power_weight(test_data, show_labels = TRUE)   without_labels <- plot_power_weight(test_data, show_labels = FALSE)    expect_s3_class(with_labels, "ggplot")   expect_s3_class(without_labels, "ggplot") })  test_that("plot_mpg_by_cylinder returns ggplot object", {   test_data <- mtcars[1:5, ]    result <- plot_mpg_by_cylinder(test_data)    expect_s3_class(result, "ggplot") })

title: Quickstart: Collaborative Analysis Workflow
text: Edit analysis/report/report.Rmd include new visualizations. Add following sections existing histogram: Update Conclusions section:
code: ### Power vs Weight Analysis   ``` r # Functions loaded via devtools::load_all() in setup chunk plot_power_weight(data, show_labels = TRUE) ```  This scatter plot reveals the relationship between engine power and vehicle weight. Higher efficiency vehicles (green) cluster in the lower-left region, indicating lower weight and moderate horsepower.  ### Cylinder Comparison   ``` r plot_mpg_by_cylinder(data) ```  The boxplot demonstrates the expected inverse relationship between cylinder count and fuel efficiency. Four-cylinder vehicles show the highest MPG values, while eight-cylinder vehicles demonstrate lower fuel efficiency. ## Conclusions  This analysis of the mtcars subset reveals several key findings:  1. **Efficiency Classification**: The dataset contains a mix of low, medium,    and high efficiency vehicles based on the 15 and 20 MPG thresholds.  2. **Weight-Power Relationship**: There is a clear positive correlation    between vehicle weight and horsepower, with efficiency class serving as    a useful categorization.  3. **Cylinder Effect**: Vehicles with fewer cylinders consistently achieve    better fuel economy, supporting the expected engineering trade-off between    power and efficiency.  ## Session Information   ``` r sessionInfo() ```

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Reproducibility computational research exists spectrum. guide presents progressive, five-level approach reproducibility using ZZCOLLAB, allowing researchers adopt tools incrementally based project needs collaboration requirements. level adds specific guarantees reproducibility introducing additional complexity. Understanding tradeoffs enables informed decisions appropriate reproducibility investments different research contexts.

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Level 1: Basic R Project - Manual package management Level 2: renv - Automated dependency tracking Level 3: renv + Docker - Complete environment isolation Level 4: renv + Docker + Unit Testing - Computational correctness Level 5: renv + Docker + Unit Testing + CI/CD - Automated validation

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: standard R project structure manual package management. Packages installed user’s global R library using install.packages().

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: my-project/ ├── .Rproj ├── R/ │   └── functions.R ├── data/ │   └── raw_data/ ├── scripts/ │   └── 01_analysis.R └── README.md

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: # Create project in RStudio: File > New Project > New Directory > New Project # Or from command line: dir.create("my-project") setwd("my-project") dir.create(c("R", "data/raw_data", "scripts"))

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: # Install packages globally install.packages("dplyr") install.packages("ggplot2")  # Load packages in scripts library(dplyr) library(ggplot2)  # Perform analysis data %>%   filter(condition) %>%   ggplot(aes(x, y)) + geom_point()

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Simple: additional tools concepts learn Fast setup: Start coding immediately Flexible: Easy experiment new packages Low overhead: configuration files maintain Standard workflow: Familiar R users

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: version tracking: Package versions recorded Update fragility: update.packages() can break existing code Collaboration friction: “Works machine” problems common Time-dependent failure: Code may stop working months later due package updates Implicit dependencies: record packages actually required System-dependent: Results may differ across operating systems

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Appropriate : Personal exploratory analysis Short-lived projects (< 1 week) Learning R packages Quick prototypes intended sharing Analysis revisit appropriate : Published research Team collaboration Long-term projects Code ’ll share others

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: # January 2024: Code works library(dplyr) data %>% select(x, y) %>% filter(x > 0)  # dplyr 1.1.0  # June 2024: After update.packages() # dplyr 1.2.0 introduces breaking change # Code produces different results or fails

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: renv package creates project-specific R libraries records exact package versions lockfile. project isolated dependencies independent global R library.

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: my-project/ ├── .Rproj ├── renv/                    # Project-specific package library ├── renv.lock               # Exact package versions (auto-generated) ├── .Rprofile               # Auto-activates renv (auto-generated) ├── R/ ├── data/ ├── scripts/ └── README.md

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: # Initialize renv in existing project install.packages("renv")  # One-time global install renv::init()  # Or create new project with ZZCOLLAB mkdir my-project && cd my-project zzc minimal                      # Minimal profile includes renv

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: # Install packages (now project-specific) # Both methods work - install.packages() is simpler in ZZCOLLAB Docker containers install.packages("dplyr") install.packages("ggplot2") # For GitHub packages: remotes::install_github("user/package")  # Work normally library(dplyr) library(ggplot2)  # Save dependency state # In ZZCOLLAB Docker containers: snapshot happens automatically on exit! # Outside Docker: run renv::snapshot() manually  # Collaborator restores exact versions renv::restore()   # Installs from renv.lock

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Version locking: Exact package versions recorded renv.lock Project isolation: Projects interfere Collaboration support: Team members get identical package versions Explicit dependencies: renv.lock documents required packages Time resilience: Project works months/years later packages Safe updates: Test updates isolation committing Minimal learning curve: Familiar R workflow install.packages()

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Additional storage: project stores package copies (~100-500MB typical) Initial setup time: First renv::restore() downloads packages Snapshot discipline: Must remember renv::snapshot() package changes (automated ZZCOLLAB Docker containers - see Level 3) R version dependency: Still depends user’s R version System library dependency: Still depends system libraries (gdal, geos, etc.) Platform differences: Windows vs Mac vs Linux can still cause issues

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Appropriate : Research projects intended publication Team collaboration (2+ researchers) Long-term projects (> 1 month) Code shared publicly Reproducible analysis workflows Still insufficient : Cross-platform guarantees (Windows/Mac/Linux differences) System library dependencies (geospatial, databases) Complete environment isolation Exact R version requirements

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Guaranteed: R package versions across users/time guaranteed: R version, system libraries, OS

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: # Day 1: Start project renv::init() install.packages("tidyverse") renv::snapshot()  # Day 30: Add new analysis install.packages("lme4") # ... write code ... renv::snapshot()  # Remember to snapshot!  # Note: With ZZCOLLAB Docker (Level 3), snapshot happens automatically on container exit!  # Year 2: Collaborator joins git clone project # In R: renv::restore()  # Gets exact package versions from Day 30

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Docker containers provide complete environment isolation including R version, system libraries, operating system. Everything runs identical Linux environment regardless host operating system.

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: my-project/ ├── .Rproj ├── renv/ ├── renv.lock ├── Dockerfile              # Environment definition ├── docker-compose.yml      # Container orchestration ├── .dockerignore           # Files to exclude from container ├── Makefile                # Convenient Docker commands ├── R/ ├── data/ ├── scripts/ └── README.md

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: # Create project with Docker environment mkdir my-project && cd my-project zzc minimal                      # Full setup (init + renv + docker)  # Or add Docker to existing renv project # (requires manual Dockerfile creation)

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: # Start development environment make r  # Now inside container (identical Linux environment) R > install.packages("tidyverse")  # Standard R command works! > # ... work normally ... > quit()  # Exit container - AUTO-SNAPSHOT HAPPENS AUTOMATICALLY! exit  # Auto-snapshot architecture (October 2025) # - renv::snapshot() runs automatically on container exit # - No manual snapshot command required! # - renv.lock automatically updated and validated (pure shell)  # Collaborator gets identical environment git clone project make r  # Builds identical container

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Complete isolation: R version, packages, system libraries controlled Cross-platform consistency: environment Windows/Mac/Linux System dependency management: GDAL, GEOS, database drivers included Reproducible system state: Dockerfile version-controls entire environment Development/production parity: environment locally servers Easy collaboration: “Pull run” - local R installation needed Multiple R versions: Different projects can use different R versions

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Storage overhead: Docker images 800MB-3GB per project Learning curve: Docker concepts (images, containers, volumes) Initial setup time: Building Docker image (2-20 minutes depending mode) Resource usage: Docker daemon must run background File permission complexity: User IDs can cause permission issues Slower package installation: First install container takes longer Platform limitations: ARM64 (Apple Silicon) vs AMD64 compatibility issues

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Appropriate : Cross-platform collaboration (Windows/Mac/Linux teams) Projects system dependencies (geospatial, databases) Research requiring exact R version specification Complex environment dependencies Production deployment analysis pipelines Long-term archival (10+ years) Overkill : Solo projects single platform Simple analysis dependencies Short-term exploratory work

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Guaranteed: R version, packages, system libraries, OS guaranteed: Code correctness, analytical validity, absence bugs

what: The Critical Gap: Environment vs Correctness
title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Docker ensures everyone runs code identical environment, ensure code produces correct results. reproducibility paradox: Level 3 guarantees consistency correctness. Level 4 addresses gap.
code: # Everyone gets the same WRONG answer in Docker calculate_mean <- function(x) {   sum(x)  # BUG: forgot to divide by length }  # Reproducible? Yes - same environment, same (wrong) result # Correct? No - analytical error

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: # Team lead creates environment with geospatial profile mkdir climate-study && cd climate-study zzc geospatial                   # Full setup with geospatial profile zzc github                       # Create private GitHub repo  # Profile provides: # - R version: latest stable # - System libraries: gdal, geos, proj # - Base packages: tidyverse, sf, terra, raster  # Build and share team image (installs packages from renv.lock) make docker-build make docker-push-team            # Optional but efficient for team  # Team member joins git clone https://github.com/mylab/climate-study cd climate-study make docker-build               # Build from project Dockerfile make r  # Inside container - everyone has same environment R --version  # Same R version for all sf::sf_extSoftVersion()  # Same GDAL version for all

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: ZZCOLLAB provides 14+ predefined Docker profiles balance image size capabilities:
code: # Minimal: Essential R development (~780MB) zzc minimal  # Analysis: Data analysis with tidyverse (~1.2GB) [RECOMMENDED] zzc analysis  # Geospatial: GIS and spatial analysis (~1.5GB) zzc geospatial  # Bioinformatics: Genomics and proteomics (~1.98GB) zzc bioinformatics  # Alpine Minimal: Lightweight (~200MB) zzc alpine_minimal  # List all available profiles zzc help profiles

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Unit testing validates code produces correct results, just consistent results. Docker ensures environment, tests ensure analysis analytically sound, free logical errors, robust edge cases.

previous_headings: Level 4: renv + Docker + Unit Testing (Computational Correctness)
title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Research demonstrates 50-89% published studies fail replication attempts, often due computational errors testing catch: --one errors data indexing Incorrect statistical calculations Silent data quality issues (missing values, outliers) Function side effects state dependencies Edge cases handled data processing Unit testing transforms implicit assumptions explicit, validated specifications.

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: # Start development environment make r  # Inside container: Test-Driven Development cycle R > # 1. Write test first (documents expected behavior) > usethis::use_test("data-preparation")  # tests/testthat/test-data-preparation.R > # Write failing test > test_that("prepare_data removes rows with missing body_mass_g", { >   library(palmerpenguins) >   result <- prepare_data(penguins) >   expect_false(anyNA(result$body_mass_g)) > })  > # 2. Run test (fails - function doesn't exist yet) > devtools::test()  > # 3. Implement function to make test pass > usethis::use_r("data_functions")  # R/data_functions.R > prepare_data <- function(data) { >   data %>% >     filter(!is.na(body_mass_g)) > }  > # 4. Run test again (passes) > devtools::test()  > # 5. Refactor with confidence > quit() exit  # Validate in clean environment make docker-test

text: Scenario: Analyze relationship bill length body mass log transformation.

previous_headings: Level 4: renv + Docker + Unit Testing (Computational Correctness) > Test-Driven Data Analysis Workflow > Example: Palmer Penguins Analysis
what: Step 1: Write Tests for Data Preparation
title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: # tests/testthat/test-data-preparation.R library(testthat) library(palmerpenguins)  test_that("prepare_penguin_data removes missing values", {   result <- prepare_penguin_data(penguins)    # No missing values in analysis variables   expect_false(anyNA(result$body_mass_g))   expect_false(anyNA(result$bill_length_mm)) })  test_that("prepare_penguin_data creates log transformation", {   result <- prepare_penguin_data(penguins)    # Log transformation exists   expect_true("log_body_mass_g" %in% names(result))    # Log transformation is correct   expect_equal(     result$log_body_mass_g,     log(result$body_mass_g)   ) })  test_that("prepare_penguin_data handles edge cases", {   # Empty data   empty_data <- penguins[0, ]   result <- prepare_penguin_data(empty_data)   expect_equal(nrow(result), 0)    # All missing data   all_na <- penguins   all_na$body_mass_g <- NA   result <- prepare_penguin_data(all_na)   expect_equal(nrow(result), 0) })

previous_headings: Level 4: renv + Docker + Unit Testing (Computational Correctness) > Test-Driven Data Analysis Workflow > Example: Palmer Penguins Analysis
what: Step 2: Implement Function to Pass Tests
title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: # R/data_functions.R  #' Prepare penguin data for analysis #' #' Removes missing values and creates log-transformed body mass variable. #' #' @param data Data frame with penguin measurements #' @return Data frame with complete cases and log_body_mass_g variable #' @export prepare_penguin_data <- function(data) {   data %>%     filter(!is.na(body_mass_g), !is.na(bill_length_mm)) %>%     mutate(log_body_mass_g = log(body_mass_g)) }

previous_headings: Level 4: renv + Docker + Unit Testing (Computational Correctness) > Test-Driven Data Analysis Workflow > Example: Palmer Penguins Analysis
what: Step 3: Write Tests for Statistical Analysis
title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: # tests/testthat/test-analysis.R test_that("fit_penguin_model returns valid lm object", {   test_data <- prepare_penguin_data(penguins)   model <- fit_penguin_model(test_data)    # Valid lm object   expect_s3_class(model, "lm")    # Correct variables in model   expect_true("bill_length_mm" %in% names(coef(model)))    # Model has reasonable fit (R-squared > 0)   expect_gt(summary(model)$r.squared, 0) })  test_that("fit_penguin_model coefficients are sensible", {   test_data <- prepare_penguin_data(penguins)   model <- fit_penguin_model(test_data)    # Positive relationship expected (larger bills -> larger penguins)   expect_gt(coef(model)["bill_length_mm"], 0) })

code: # R/analysis_functions.R  #' Fit linear model of log body mass vs bill length #' #' @param data Prepared penguin data #' @return Linear model object #' @export fit_penguin_model <- function(data) {   lm(log_body_mass_g ~ bill_length_mm, data = data) }

what: Step 5: Write Tests for Data Quality
title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: # tests/testthat/test-data-quality.R test_that("penguin data has expected structure", {   # Required columns exist   required_cols <- c("species", "island", "bill_length_mm",                      "bill_depth_mm", "body_mass_g")   expect_true(all(required_cols %in% names(penguins)))    # Reasonable sample size   expect_gt(nrow(penguins), 300)    # Species are valid   expect_true(all(penguins$species %in%                     c("Adelie", "Chinstrap", "Gentoo"))) })  test_that("body mass measurements are plausible", {   valid_data <- penguins %>% filter(!is.na(body_mass_g))    # Body mass in plausible range (2500g to 6500g)   expect_true(all(valid_data$body_mass_g >= 2500))   expect_true(all(valid_data$body_mass_g <= 6500))    # No negative values   expect_true(all(valid_data$body_mass_g > 0)) })

previous_headings: Level 4: renv + Docker + Unit Testing (Computational Correctness) > Testing Best Practices for Research
title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: test_that("analysis handles edge cases gracefully", {   # Single observation   single_obs <- data.frame(x = 1, y = 2)   expect_error(fit_model(single_obs), "Insufficient data")    # No variance   no_variance <- data.frame(x = c(1, 1, 1), y = c(2, 3, 4))   expect_warning(fit_model(no_variance), "No variance in predictor")    # Perfect correlation   perfect_cor <- data.frame(x = 1:10, y = 1:10)   result <- fit_model(perfect_cor)   expect_equal(summary(result)$r.squared, 1.0) })

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Research Stodden et al. (2018) found: 70% researchers tried failed reproduce another scientist’s experiments 50% failed reproduce experiments Computational errors leading cause replication failures

previous_headings: Level 4: renv + Docker + Unit Testing (Computational Correctness) > Scientific Motivation for Testing
title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: study GitHub projects (Mockus, 2010) demonstrated: Projects >80% test coverage 40% fewer defects Test-driven development reduced debugging time 16% Tested code 24% higher change success rate

previous_headings: Level 4: renv + Docker + Unit Testing (Computational Correctness) > Scientific Motivation for Testing
title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: # Without tests: Silent error propagates analyze_data <- function(data) {   # BUG: Uses sd() instead of var() for variance   variance <- sd(data$x)  # WRONG    # Analysis appears to work but results are incorrect   result <- mean(data$x) / variance   return(result) }  # With tests: Error caught immediately test_that("variance calculation is correct", {   test_data <- data.frame(x = c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5))    # This test fails, exposing the bug   result <- calculate_variance(test_data$x)   expect_equal(result, var(test_data$x))  # FAILS })

previous_headings: Level 4: renv + Docker + Unit Testing (Computational Correctness) > Common Testing Mistakes
title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: # Insufficient test test_that("function works", {   result <- my_function(data)   expect_true(is.data.frame(result))  # Only tests type, not correctness })  # Better test test_that("function produces correct transformations", {   test_data <- data.frame(x = c(1, 2, 3), y = c(4, 5, 6))   result <- my_function(test_data)    # Test specific expected behavior   expect_equal(result$x_squared, c(1, 4, 9))   expect_equal(result$y_doubled, c(8, 10, 12))   expect_equal(nrow(result), 3) })

previous_headings: Level 4: renv + Docker + Unit Testing (Computational Correctness) > Common Testing Mistakes
title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: # Wrong: Tests implementation details test_that("function uses dplyr::filter", {   # Breaks if you refactor to base R   expect_true(exists("filter", envir = environment(my_function))) })  # Right: Tests behavior test_that("function removes missing values", {   # Passes regardless of implementation   test_data <- data.frame(x = c(1, NA, 3))   result <- my_function(test_data)   expect_false(anyNA(result)) })

previous_headings: Level 4: renv + Docker + Unit Testing (Computational Correctness) > Common Testing Mistakes
title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: # Incomplete testing test_that("mean calculation works", {   expect_equal(calculate_mean(c(1, 2, 3)), 2) })  # Complete testing includes edge cases test_that("mean calculation handles edge cases", {   # Normal case   expect_equal(calculate_mean(c(1, 2, 3)), 2)    # Single value   expect_equal(calculate_mean(5), 5)    # Empty vector   expect_true(is.nan(calculate_mean(numeric(0))))    # Missing values   expect_equal(calculate_mean(c(1, NA, 3), na.rm = TRUE), 2) })

what: When Testing Is Not Worth It
title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Testing costs. Skip testing : One-time exploratory scripts reused Simple visualizations calculations Wrapper functions call tested functions Interactive analysis share Focus testing effort : Data processing pipelines complex logic Statistical calculations beyond simple base R functions Functions used across multiple analyses Code shared published

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Write tests incrementally: Validate dependencies committing: Use descriptive commit messages:
code: # tests/testthat/test-data-preparation.R test_that("prepare_data removes missing values", {   raw <- data.frame(x = c(1, NA, 3), y = c(4, 5, 6))   result <- prepare_data(raw)   expect_equal(nrow(result), 2)   expect_false(anyNA(result)) }) # Check that all packages used in code are declared (pure shell - NO R REQUIRED!) make check-renv  # Pure shell validation (grep, awk, jq)  # This prevents CI failures due to missing packages # Good: Helps understand CI failure context git commit -m "Add bootstrapping to uncertainty analysis"  # Bad: Unclear what CI is testing git commit -m "Update code"

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Projects can migrate progressively: need commit Level 5 immediately - start Level 2, add Docker needed cross-platform work, add tests analysis complexity grows, add CI/CD team grows.
code: Level 1 → Level 2:   renv::init()  # 5 minutes  Level 2 → Level 3:   zzc minimal                    # Quick minimal setup   zzc analysis                   # Or full analysis environment  Level 3 → Level 4:   # Inside Docker container   usethis::use_testthat()  # Initialize testing   # Write tests for critical functions   # 1-2 days for comprehensive test suite  Level 4 → Level 5:   zzc github                     # Adds GitHub workflows (or already done)

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Scenario: Testing new statistical method small dataset. Duration: 2 days Recommended Level: Level 1 Rationale: Quick iteration important reproducibility. method shows promise, migrate Level 2 thorough analysis.
code: # Just use global packages install.packages("bootstrap") library(bootstrap)  # Analyze and move on

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Scenario: Student analyzing dataset thesis. share code advisor. May extend analysis publication. Duration: 3-6 months Recommended Level: Level 2 Rationale: Need ensure advisor can reproduce results. Long enough packages might update analysis.
code: # Initialize renv renv::init() install.packages("tidyverse") install.packages("lme4")  # Analysis code...  # Snapshot before sharing with advisor renv::snapshot()  # Advisor runs: renv::restore()  # Gets exact package versions

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Scenario: Three universities collaborating climate analysis. Different platforms (Windows, Mac, Linux). Complex geospatial dependencies. Custom spatial interpolation algorithms. Duration: 2 years Recommended Level: Level 4 Rationale: Cross-platform team requires Docker. Complex custom algorithms require unit tests ensure correctness. Geospatial packages (sf, terra) complex system dependencies.
code: # Team lead setup with geospatial profile mkdir precipitation && cd precipitation zzc geospatial                   # Full setup with geospatial profile zzc github                       # Create private GitHub repo  # Build and share team image (installs packages from renv.lock) make docker-build make docker-push-team            # Optional but efficient for team  # Add testing infrastructure make r R > usethis::use_testthat() > # Write tests for spatial interpolation algorithms > # Write tests for data quality validation > quit() exit  # Team members (any platform) git clone https://github.com/climate-team/precipitation cd precipitation make docker-build               # Build from project Dockerfile make r  # Identical environment on all platforms  # Inside container - everyone has same GDAL, GEOS versions # Tests validate spatial algorithms work correctly

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Scenario: Developing R package CRAN submission. Contributors around world. Must pass R CMD check Windows/Mac/Linux. Duration: Ongoing Recommended Level: Level 5 Rationale: Multiple contributors require automated testing. CRAN submission requires validation multiple platforms. CI/CD ensures every contribution passes checks merge.
code: # Package maintainer setup mkdir mypackage && cd mypackage zzc analysis                     # Full setup (init + renv + docker) zzc github                       # Create private GitHub repo with CI/CD  # Testing and CI/CD included automatically # Every contributor push triggers: # - R CMD check on Ubuntu # - Test suite execution (unit tests) # - Documentation validation # - Dependency checking  # Pull requests show CI status # Maintainer only merges if CI passes

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Scenario: Automated weekly report generation database. Runs server. Code rarely changes must always work. Report includes statistical summaries data quality checks. Duration: Ongoing Recommended Level: Level 4 Rationale: Production deployment requires Docker. Statistical calculations data quality checks tested prevent silent errors automated reports. CI/CD (Level 5) optional code changes infrequently.
code: # Setup with Docker for production (minimal profile sufficient for reporting) mkdir weekly-report && cd weekly-report zzc minimal                      # Full setup with minimal profile  # Add tests for report calculations make r R > usethis::use_testthat() > # Write tests for: > # - Statistical summaries (means, medians, etc.) > # - Data quality checks (missing data, outliers) > # - Report formatting functions > quit() exit  # Deploy to server docker build -t weekly-report . docker run -v /data:/data weekly-report Rscript generate_report.R  # Optional: Add CI/CD (Level 5) if code complexity grows or team expands zzc github                       # Creates GitHub repo with CI/CD workflows

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: renv creates project-specific library : Installing packages renv/library/ instead global library Recording versions renv.lock (JSON format) Activating via .Rprofile sets .libPaths()
code: # Before renv::init() .libPaths() # [1] "/usr/local/lib/R/site-library"  # Global library  # After renv::init() .libPaths() # [1] "/project/renv/library/R-4.3/x86_64-pc-linux-gnu"  # Project library # [2] "/usr/local/lib/R/site-library"                    # Fallback

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Docker creates isolated environment : Base image (rocker/r-ver:4.3.1) - specific R version System libraries (GDAL, GEOS, etc.) installed via apt-get R packages installed via Dockerfile instructions Project files mounted volume Container runs isolated process filesystem, project directory mounted live editing.
code: FROM rocker/r-ver:4.3.1 RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y libgdal-dev RUN R -e "install.packages('renv')" WORKDIR /project

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: GitHub Actions validates : Checking code repository Building Docker container Dockerfile Running validation scripts container Reporting results via GitHub interface
code: # .github/workflows/r-package.yml jobs:   check:     runs-on: ubuntu-latest     container: rocker/tidyverse:latest     steps:       - uses: actions/checkout@v4       - name: Validate dependencies (pure shell - NO R REQUIRED!)         run: make check-renv  # Pure shell validation (grep, awk, jq)

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Forgetting renv::snapshot() (Note: Automated ZZCOLLAB Docker!): Package installation methods (work ZZCOLLAB Docker containers): Note: ZZCOLLAB Docker containers, install.packages() works correctly packages automatically captured renv.lock exit container.
code: # Wrong workflow (manual renv without Docker) install.packages("new-package") # ... use package in code ... git commit  # renv.lock not updated!  # Collaborator runs: renv::restore()  # Doesn't get new-package # Code fails with "package not found"  # Correct workflow (manual renv) install.packages("new-package") renv::snapshot()  # Update renv.lock git add renv.lock git commit  # ZZCOLLAB Docker auto-snapshot (October 2025): # With ZZCOLLAB Docker containers, snapshot happens automatically on exit! make r install.packages("new-package")  # Standard R command works in Docker exit  # Auto-snapshot runs automatically - no manual command needed! git add renv.lock && git commit # Standard R command (recommended in ZZCOLLAB Docker containers) install.packages("dplyr")  # Auto-captured in renv.lock on exit  # For GitHub packages install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("user/package")

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: File permission issues: Forgetting exit container git operations: Editing files outside container: Files edited host (Mac/Windows) visible container, editors create temp files can cause issues. Best practice: edit host, run container.
code: # Inside container (running as root) touch output.csv  # Outside container (your user) rm output.csv  # Permission denied!  # Solution: Container should run as non-root user (zzcollab does this) # Wrong make r git commit  # Runs inside container  # Right make r # ... work ... exit  # Exit container first git commit  # Run on host

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: testing edge cases: Testing implementation details instead behavior: Forgetting run tests committing:
code: # Inadequate test test_that("data preparation works", {   result <- prepare_data(penguins)   expect_true(is.data.frame(result)) })  # Better test includes edge cases test_that("data preparation handles edge cases", {   # Normal case   result <- prepare_data(penguins)   expect_equal(nrow(result), 333)    # Empty data   expect_equal(nrow(prepare_data(penguins[0, ])), 0)    # All missing values   all_na <- penguins   all_na$body_mass_g <- NA   expect_equal(nrow(prepare_data(all_na)), 0) }) # Wrong: Tests how function is implemented test_that("uses dplyr for filtering", {   # Breaks if you switch to base R   expect_match(deparse(body(prepare_data)), "filter") })  # Right: Tests what function does test_that("removes rows with missing body mass", {   # Works regardless of implementation   result <- prepare_data(penguins)   expect_false(anyNA(result$body_mass_g)) }) # Wrong workflow git commit -m "Update analysis" make docker-test  # Fails - should have tested first  # Right workflow make docker-test  # Test first git commit -m "Update analysis"

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: testing locally pushing: Ignoring CI failures: CI failures must fixed immediately. Ignoring leads : - Broken main branch - Inability merge pull requests - Loss confidence tests
code: # Wrong workflow git commit -m "Fix analysis" git push  # CI fails, must fix and push again  # Right workflow (pure shell validation - NO R REQUIRED!) make check-renv  # Pure shell validation (grep, awk, jq) make docker-test git commit -m "Fix analysis" git push  # CI passes

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Reproducibility binary - exists spectrum. ZZCOLLAB provides five levels increasing reproducibility guarantees: Level 1 (Manual): Quick start, guarantees Level 2 (renv): Package version guarantees Level 3 (renv + Docker): Complete environment guarantees Level 4 (renv + Docker + Unit Testing): Computational correctness guarantees Level 5 (renv + Docker + Unit Testing + CI/CD): Automated validation guarantees

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: level addresses different dimension: Environment Reproducibility (Levels 2-3): packages, R version, system libraries Computational Correctness (Level 4): Code produces analytically sound results Automated Verification (Level 5): Continuous validation everything still works

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Choose level appropriate project’s: Duration: Longer projects need reproducibility Team size: Larger teams need validation Platform diversity: Mixed platforms require Docker Analytical complexity: Complex calculations require tests Publication requirements: Published research needs high reproducibility Maintenance needs: Long-term projects need automated checks

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Start simple migrate incrementally needs evolve: Week 1: Level 1 exploration Month 1: Add Level 2 (renv) committing project Month 2: Add Level 3 (Docker) collaborating cross-platform Month 3: Add Level 4 (Testing) analysis complexity grows Month 6: Add Level 5 (CI/CD) team expands goal reproducibility without unnecessary complexity. level adds value, also adds overhead. Match reproducibility investment project requirements.

title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: vignette explains create reusable team Docker images reduce project initialization time. Rather building new Docker image rocker base images project (15-20 minutes comprehensive builds), can: Build : Create comprehensive team image preferred packages Push Docker Hub: Store image central repository Reuse everywhere: New projects use team image base (~30 seconds setup) approach particularly valuable : Research labs multiple ongoing projects Teams requiring consistent package ecosystems Solo researchers working across multiple analyses Organizations standardized R environments

title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
code: # Project 1: Build from rocker/rstudio (15-20 minutes) mkdir study1 && cd study1 zzc analysis                     # Full setup (init + renv + docker) zzc github                       # Create private GitHub repo make docker-build make docker-push-team cd ..  # Project 2: Build from rocker/rstudio again (15-20 minutes) mkdir study2 && cd study2 zzc analysis zzc github make docker-build make docker-push-team cd ..  # Project 3: Build from rocker/rstudio again (15-20 minutes) mkdir study3 && cd study3 zzc analysis zzc github make docker-build make docker-push-team  # Total time for 3 projects: 45-60 minutes

title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
code: # One-time: Build analysis team base image (15-20 minutes) mkdir baseimage && cd baseimage zzc analysis                     # Full setup with analysis profile zzc github                       # Create private GitHub repo make docker-build make docker-push-team            # Push to Docker Hub for team access git add . git commit -m "Create team base image with comprehensive packages" git push cd ..  # Project 1: Build from shared Dockerfile (~5 minutes first time) mkdir study1 && cd study1 make docker-build               # Builds from project Dockerfile cd ..  # Project 2: Build from shared Dockerfile (faster with Docker cache) mkdir study2 && cd study2 make docker-build cd ..  # Project 3: Build from shared Dockerfile (faster with Docker cache) mkdir study3 && cd study3 make docker-build

title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: project rebuilds rocker base images, reinstalling packages.
code: New Project → rocker/rstudio:latest → Install 47 packages → Team Image               (Docker Hub)              (15-20 minutes)      (mylab/study1core-rstudio:latest)

title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: New projects use pre-built team image base, avoiding package reinstallation.
code: One-Time Setup:   rocker/rstudio:latest → Install 47 packages → Team Base Image   (Docker Hub)            (15-20 minutes)       (mylab/baseimageecore-rstudio:latest)                                                  ↓ (push to Docker Hub)  Subsequent Projects:   mylab/baseimageecore-rstudio:latest → Project-Specific Setup → Project Image   (Docker Hub, pre-built)                (~30 seconds)            (mylab/study1core-rstudio:latest)

what: Step 1: Create Comprehensive Team Base Image
title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: team lead creates one-time base image preferred packages. : - Creates project structure DESCRIPTION, Makefile, etc. - Builds rocker/rstudio:latest - Installs analysis profile packages (tidyverse, , testthat, knitr, rmarkdown) - Creates mylab/baseimage:latest - Pushes Docker Hub team access Profile options: Specialized domain profiles:
code: # Create analysis team base image # This takes 15-20 minutes but only needs to be done once mkdir baseimage && cd baseimage zzc analysis                     # Full setup with analysis profile zzc github                       # Create private GitHub repo make docker-build                # Installs packages from renv.lock make docker-push-team            # Push to Docker Hub for team git add . git commit -m "Create comprehensive team base image" git push cd .. # Analysis profile (recommended for general team bases) mkdir baseimage && cd baseimage zzc analysis                     # Full setup zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team git add . && git commit -m "Create analysis team base image" && git push cd ..  # Modeling profile (machine learning focused) mkdir modeling-base && cd modeling-base zzc modeling                     # Full setup with modeling profile zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team git add . && git commit -m "Create modeling team base image" && git push cd ..  # Minimal profile (lightweight) mkdir minimal-base && cd minimal-base zzc minimal                      # Full setup with minimal profile zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team git add . && git commit -m "Create minimal team base image" && git push cd .. # Bioinformatics (Bioconductor genomics) mkdir bio-base && cd bio-base zzc bioinformatics               # Full setup with bioinformatics profile zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team git add . && git commit -m "Create bioinformatics team base image" && git push cd ..  # Geospatial (GIS and spatial analysis) mkdir geo-base && cd geo-base zzc geospatial                   # Full setup with geospatial profile zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team git add . && git commit -m "Create geospatial team base image" && git push cd ..

what: Step 2: Verify Image on Docker Hub
title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: build completes, verify image available:
code: # Check image exists locally docker images | grep mylab/baseimage  # Verify on Docker Hub (requires login) docker manifest inspect mylab/baseimage:latest  # Or visit Docker Hub in browser # https://hub.docker.com/r/mylab/baseimage

what: Step 3: Use Team Base Image for New Projects
title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: Now team member can create new projects using team base image: : - Pulls mylab/study1:latest Docker Hub (available) - Creates project structure needed - Uses team base image foundation - Takes ~1-3 minutes instead 15-20 minutes Note: DockerHub optional efficient. team image available, building Dockerfile produces identical environment.
code: # Clone an existing project repository git clone https://github.com/mylab/study1.git cd study1  # Build Docker image from project Dockerfile make docker-build  # Start development make r

what: Example 1: Solo Researcher with Multiple Projects
title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: Dr. Smith works various analyses wants consistent environments.
code: # One-time setup: Create personal base image (Sunday afternoon) mkdir mybase && cd mybase zzc analysis                     # Full setup with analysis profile zzc github                       # Create private GitHub repo make docker-build                # Installs packages from renv.lock make docker-push-team            # Push to Docker Hub for reuse git add . && git commit -m "Create personal base image" && git push cd .. # Takes 15-20 minutes, grab coffee  # Monday: Start microbiome analysis mkdir microbiome-study && cd microbiome-study zzc analysis                     # Full setup zzc github # Ready in seconds (framework files created)  # Wednesday: Start climate modeling project mkdir climate-model && cd climate-model zzc analysis zzc github # Ready in seconds  # Friday: Start survey analysis mkdir survey-analysis && cd survey-analysis zzc analysis zzc github # Ready in seconds  # Total time: ~16 minutes vs 45-60 minutes (traditional approach)

what: Example 2: Research Lab with Team Collaboration
title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: genomics lab 5 researchers multiple ongoing studies. Team Lead (one-time setup): Team Members (joining existing projects):
code: # Create lab base image with bioinformatics tools mkdir labbase && cd labbase zzc bioinformatics               # Full setup with bioinformatics profile zzc github                       # Create private GitHub repo make docker-build                # Installs packages from renv.lock make docker-push-team            # Push to Docker Hub for team git add . && git commit -m "Create lab base image" && git push cd ..  # The bioinformatics profile includes Bioconductor base image and packages # Additional packages can be added via install.packages() as needed # Researcher 1: Join existing cancer genomics project git clone https://github.com/genomicslab/cancer-study.git cd cancer-study make docker-build               # Build from project Dockerfile make docker-rstudio              # Start RStudio at localhost:8787  # Researcher 2: Join existing RNA-seq analysis git clone https://github.com/genomicslab/rnaseq-analysis.git cd rnaseq-analysis make docker-build make docker-rstudio  # Researcher 3: Create new variant calling project mkdir variant-calling && cd variant-calling zzc bioinformatics               # Full setup with bioinformatics profile zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team make docker-rstudio  # All 3 researchers ready in ~90 seconds total # Traditional approach: 45-60 minutes total

title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: data science consulting firm requires consistent package versions across client projects. DevOps Team (quarterly base image updates): Data Scientists (creating client projects):
code: # Q1 2025: Create standardized base image mkdir q1-2025-base && cd q1-2025-base zzc analysis                     # Full setup with analysis profile zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team git add . && git commit -m "Create Q1 2025 standardized base image" && git push cd ..  # Push to private Docker registry (optional) docker tag datasci-firm/q1-2025-base:latest \   registry.company.com/datasci-firm/q1-2025-base:latest docker push registry.company.com/datasci-firm/q1-2025-base:latest # Client A: Retail analytics mkdir client-a-retail && cd client-a-retail zzc analysis                     # Full setup zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team  # Client B: Financial modeling mkdir client-b-finance && cd client-b-finance zzc analysis zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team  # Client C: Healthcare dashboards mkdir client-c-health && cd client-c-health zzc analysis zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team  # All projects use identical Q1 2025 package versions # No "works on my machine" issues

title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: Create different base images different research domains:
code: # Bioinformatics base (Bioconductor packages) mkdir bio-base && cd bio-base zzc bioinformatics               # Full setup with bioinformatics profile zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team git add . && git commit -m "Create bioinformatics base image" && git push cd .. # Use for: genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics projects  # Geospatial base (sf, terra, raster) mkdir geo-base && cd geo-base zzc geospatial                   # Full setup with geospatial profile zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team git add . && git commit -m "Create geospatial base image" && git push cd .. # Use for: climate modeling, ecology, urban planning projects  # Machine learning base (modeling packages) mkdir ml-base && cd ml-base zzc modeling                     # Full setup with modeling profile zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team git add . && git commit -m "Create machine learning base image" && git push cd .. # Use for: predictive modeling, classification, regression projects  # Publishing base (LaTeX, Quarto, rmarkdown) mkdir pub-base && cd pub-base zzc publishing                   # Full setup with publishing profile zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team git add . && git commit -m "Create publishing base image" && git push cd .. # Use for: manuscripts, reports, reproducible documents

title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: Maintain version history reproducibility:
code: # Create dated base image mkdir base-2025-01 && cd base-2025-01 zzc analysis                     # Full setup with analysis profile zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team git add . && git commit -m "Create January 2025 versioned base image" && git push cd ..  # Tag with semantic version docker tag mylab/base-2025-01:latest \   mylab/base-2025-01:v1.0.0 docker push mylab/base-2025-01:v1.0.0  # Projects can reference specific versions # Modify project Dockerfile to use: # FROM mylab/base-2025-01:v1.0.0

what: Pattern 3: Incremental Updates to Base Images
title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: Update base images without rebuilding scratch:
code: # Add new package to existing base image docker run --rm -it mylab/baseimage:latest R  # Inside container: install.packages("newpackage") quit()  # Commit changes to new version docker commit <container-id> mylab/baseimage:v1.1.0 docker push mylab/baseimage:v1.1.0  # Or rebuild base image with updated packages cd baseimage make docker-build make docker-push-team # Leverages Docker layer caching for faster rebuilds

title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: Public repositories: Unlimited, free forever Private repositories: 1 private repo free tier Image pulls: Rate limits apply (200 pulls per 6 hours free tier)

title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: public research (recommended): private/proprietary work:
code: # Use public repositories (no cost) mkdir baseimage && cd baseimage zzc analysis                     # Full setup with analysis profile zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team git add . && git commit -m "Create public team base image" && git push # Pushes to public mylab/baseimage:latest # No storage costs, unlimited team access # Option 1: Docker Hub paid tier ($7/month for unlimited private repos) # Option 2: Self-hosted registry (GitLab, Harbor, etc.) # Option 3: Cloud provider registries (AWS ECR, Google GCR, Azure ACR)

title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: Comprehensive builds create large images (~2-4 GB). Optimize storage:
code: # Check image sizes docker images | grep mylab/baseimage  # Remove old versions periodically docker rmi mylab/baseimage:v1.0.0  # Use .dockerignore to exclude unnecessary files echo "*.Rcheck" >> .dockerignore echo "temp_*" >> .dockerignore

title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: Quarterly: Recommended teams (balances stability vs currency) Monthly: teams requiring latest package versions major releases: critical packages update (e.g., tidyverse 2.0) adding new team members: new domain expertise requires new packages

title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
code: # Rebuild base image with latest packages cd baseimage make docker-build make docker-push-team  # Tag as new version docker tag mylab/baseimage:latest \   mylab/baseimage:2025-04 docker push mylab/baseimage:2025-04  # Notify team to pull updated image docker pull mylab/baseimage:latest  # Existing projects continue using old versions (reproducible) # New projects use updated image

title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: Docker Hub free tier limits pulls 200 per 6 hours. Solutions:
code: # Option 1: Login to Docker Hub (increases limit to 200 per 6 hours per user) docker login  # Option 2: Use cached images (do not pull if local copy exists) docker images | grep mylab/baseimage # If exists locally, zzcollab uses cached version  # Option 3: Upgrade to Docker Hub paid tier (unlimited pulls)

title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
code: # Check Docker layer caching docker build --no-cache -f Dockerfile.teamcore -t test-image . # Compare with cached build: docker build -f Dockerfile.teamcore -t test-image .  # Optimize Dockerfile layer order # Put frequently changing commands last # Put stable dependencies first (leverages caching)

what: Environment Variables in Team Base Images
title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: zzcollab-generated Docker images automatically include reproducibility-critical environment variables. creating reusable team base images, verify present: Matters Team Images: team uses shared base image, team members inherit environment variable settings. ensures: Consistent string sorting across analyses Identical date-time calculations regardless team member location Reproducible parallel processing behavior locale-dependent result differences Verification: manually modify team base image Dockerfiles, ensure variables remain set. Removing can cause silent reproducibility failures across team.
code: # Reproducibility-critical environment variables (automatically included) ENV LANG=en_US.UTF-8        # Standardize locale ENV LC_ALL=en_US.UTF-8      # Override all locale categories ENV TZ=UTC                  # Eliminate daylight saving complications ENV OMP_NUM_THREADS=1       # Deterministic single-threaded execution # Check environment variables in team base image docker run --rm mylab/baseimage:latest env | grep -E "LANG|TZ|OMP"  # Expected output: # LANG=en_US.UTF-8 # LC_ALL=en_US.UTF-8 # TZ=UTC # OMP_NUM_THREADS=1

title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
code: # Good: Indicates purpose and currency mylab/bioinformatics-base-2025 mylab/datascience-comprehensive mylab/shiny-apps-standard  # Avoid: Generic names mylab/base mylab/image1 mylab/test

title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: Create BASE_IMAGE_README.md base image project:
code: # Lab Base Image: mylab/baseimage  ## Purpose Data analysis environment for lab projects.  ## Docker Profile analysis: tidyverse, here, testthat, knitr, rmarkdown  ## Key Packages - tidyverse: Data manipulation - here: Project-relative paths - testthat: Unit testing - knitr/rmarkdown: Reproducible reports  ## Last Updated 2025-01-15  ## Rebuild Command cd baseimage make docker-build make docker-push-team

what: 3. Use Semantic Versioning for Critical Bases
title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
code: # Major version: Breaking changes (R version upgrade, package removals) mylab/baseimage:v2.0.0  # Minor version: New packages added mylab/baseimage:v1.1.0  # Patch version: Package version updates only mylab/baseimage:v1.0.1

what: 4. Test Base Images Before Team Deployment
title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
code: # Build test base image mkdir baseimage-test && cd baseimage-test zzc analysis                     # Full setup with analysis profile zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team git add . && git commit -m "Create test base image for validation" && git push cd ..  # Test with sample project mkdir test-project && cd test-project zzc analysis make docker-rstudio  # Verify all key packages load # In RStudio: library(tidyverse) library(tidymodels) library(shiny) # etc.  # If tests pass, promote to production docker tag mylab/baseimage-test:latest \   mylab/baseimage:latest docker push mylab/baseimage:latest

title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: Keep centralized list available base images:
code: # team-base-images.md # Lab Base Images  | Name | Profile | Purpose | Last Updated | |------|---------|---------|--------------| | mylab/bio-base | bioinformatics | Bioconductor genomics | 2025-01-15 | | mylab/geo-base | geospatial | Geospatial analysis | 2025-01-10 | | mylab/ml-base | modeling | Machine learning | 2025-01-05 | | mylab/pub-base | publishing | LaTeX/Quarto docs | 2024-12-20 |  ## Usage # For bioinformatics projects: mkdir <project-name> && cd <project-name> zzc bioinformatics               # Full setup with bioinformatics profile zzc github                       # Create private GitHub repo

what: Should You Create a Reusable Team Base Image?
title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: Strong Yes : - work 3+ projects similar package requirements - team consistent development environment needs - frequently start new projects (monthly ) - Build time bottleneck workflow - need reproducible environments across projects Maybe : - 1-2 ongoing projects - Package requirements vary widely projects - rarely start new projects (quarterly less) - ’re experimenting different package ecosystems Probably : - Single project, infrequent updates - Extremely specialized package requirements per project - Storage constraints (Docker Hub free tier, limited disk space) - Team members different R version requirements

title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: Reusable team base images provide substantial time savings teams solo researchers working across multiple projects: Key Benefits: - 73% time savings: ~30 seconds vs 15-20 minutes per project - Consistency: Identical package versions across projects - Collaboration: Team members share exact environments - Cost-effective: Free Docker Hub public repositories Recommended Workflow: 1. Create comprehensive team base image (one-time, 15-20 minutes) 2. Push Docker Hub team access 3. Use base image new projects (~30 seconds ) 4. Update base image quarterly needed Best : - Research labs multiple ongoing projects - Solo researchers diverse analyses - Organizations requiring standardized environments - Teams needing reproducible collaboration tools approach transforms zzcollab per-project build system reusable infrastructure platform, improving developer productivity maintaining reproducibility guarantees.

previous_headings: Comprehensive Guide to Testing Data Analysis Workflows in R
title: Testing Data Analysis Workflows
text: Testing data analysis workflows presents unique challenges compared traditional software development. package development focuses testing isolated functions predictable inputs outputs, data analysis involves testing complex pipelines, validating data quality, ensuring reproducibility, verifying analytical results correct meaningful (Wilson et al., 2017; Wickham, 2015). guide provides comprehensive strategies testing data analysis workflows R, building upon established software engineering practices addressing specific needs computational research data science (Poldrack, 2019; Wilson et al., 2014).

previous_headings: Comprehensive Guide to Testing Data Analysis Workflows in R > Testing Philosophy for Data Analysis
title: Testing Data Analysis Workflows
text: Data analysis testing serves two primary goals: Computational Reproducibility: Ensuring another researcher can use code data independently obtain identical results Result Correctness: Validating results generated code accurate meaningful noted Wilson et al. (2017), “important aspect validate code using software development practices prevent errors software testing methods can help detect occur.”

previous_headings: Comprehensive Guide to Testing Data Analysis Workflows in R > Testing Philosophy for Data Analysis
title: Testing Data Analysis Workflows
text: Following reproducible research workflow principles (Poldrack, 2019), testing align three phases data analysis: Explore Phase: Rapid prototyping lightweight validation Refine Phase: Comprehensive testing stabilized analysis components Produce Phase: Full integration reproducibility testing

title: Testing Data Analysis Workflows
text: Ensure data quality consistency:
code: test_that("data meets quality standards", {   data <- load_analysis_data()      # Check data structure   expect_equal(ncol(data), 15)   expect_true(all(c("id", "outcome", "treatment") %in% names(data)))      # Check data ranges   expect_true(all(data$age >= 0 & data$age <= 120))   expect_true(all(data$outcome %in% c("success", "failure")))      # Check for missing data patterns   missing_rate <- sum(is.na(data)) / (nrow(data) * ncol(data))   expect_true(missing_rate < 0.05)  # Less than 5% missing })

code: test_that("confidence interval calculation is correct", {   # Test with known values   data <- c(10, 12, 14, 16, 18, 20)   ci <- calculate_ci(data, confidence = 0.95)      # Verify structure   expect_length(ci, 2)   expect_named(ci, c("lower", "upper"))      # Verify mathematical properties   expect_true(ci$lower < mean(data))   expect_true(ci$upper > mean(data))   expect_true(ci$upper > ci$lower) })

title: Testing Data Analysis Workflows
text: Create domain-specific validation functions:
code: validate_clinical_data <- function(data) {   test_that("clinical data validation", {     # Patient ID format     expect_true(all(grepl("^P\\d{6}$", data$patient_id)))          # Visit dates are reasonable     expect_true(all(data$visit_date >= as.Date("2020-01-01")))     expect_true(all(data$visit_date <= Sys.Date()))          # Biomarker values within expected ranges     expect_true(all(data$biomarker >= 0 & data$biomarker <= 1000))          # Treatment groups are balanced     treatment_table <- table(data$treatment)     expect_true(all(treatment_table >= 0.3 * nrow(data)))   }) }

what: Basic Data Validation Tests with iris Dataset
title: Testing Data Analysis Workflows
code: # tests/testthat/test-iris-analysis.R library(testthat)  test_that("iris dataset has expected structure", {   data(iris)      # Test basic structure   expect_equal(nrow(iris), 150)   expect_equal(ncol(iris), 5)   expect_true(all(c("Sepal.Length", "Sepal.Width", "Petal.Length",                      "Petal.Width", "Species") %in% names(iris)))      # Test data types   expect_true(is.numeric(iris$Sepal.Length))   expect_true(is.numeric(iris$Sepal.Width))   expect_true(is.factor(iris$Species))      # Test value ranges   expect_true(all(iris$Sepal.Length > 0))   expect_true(all(iris$Petal.Length >= 0))   expect_true(max(iris$Sepal.Length) <= 10)  # Reasonable upper bound      # Test species levels   expected_species <- c("setosa", "versicolor", "virginica")   expect_equal(levels(iris$Species), expected_species)   expect_equal(table(iris$Species), rep(50, 3)) })  test_that("iris measurements are biologically plausible", {   data(iris)      # Sepal measurements should be positive and reasonable   expect_true(all(iris$Sepal.Length >= 4 & iris$Sepal.Length <= 8))   expect_true(all(iris$Sepal.Width >= 1.5 & iris$Sepal.Width <= 5))      # Petal measurements   expect_true(all(iris$Petal.Length >= 1 & iris$Petal.Length <= 7))   expect_true(all(iris$Petal.Width >= 0.1 & iris$Petal.Width <= 3))      # No missing values   expect_false(any(is.na(iris))) })

previous_headings: Comprehensive Guide to Testing Data Analysis Workflows in R > Examples and Templates > Simple Examples with Built-in Datasets
title: Testing Data Analysis Workflows
code: # tests/testthat/test-mtcars-stats.R library(testthat)  # Helper function for testing calculate_mpg_summary <- function(data) {   list(     mean_mpg = mean(data$mpg),     median_mpg = median(data$mpg),     sd_mpg = sd(data$mpg),     range_mpg = range(data$mpg)   ) }  test_that("mpg summary statistics are correct", {   data(mtcars)      summary_stats <- calculate_mpg_summary(mtcars)      # Test summary statistics properties   expect_true(summary_stats$mean_mpg > 0)   expect_true(summary_stats$median_mpg > 0)   expect_true(summary_stats$sd_mpg > 0)   expect_equal(length(summary_stats$range_mpg), 2)   expect_true(summary_stats$range_mpg[1] <= summary_stats$range_mpg[2])      # Test against known values (mtcars is static)   expect_equal(summary_stats$mean_mpg, mean(mtcars$mpg))   expect_equal(summary_stats$median_mpg, median(mtcars$mpg))   # Approximate known value   expect_true(abs(summary_stats$mean_mpg - 20.09) < 0.1)   })  test_that("correlation analysis works correctly", {   data(mtcars)      # Test correlation between mpg and weight   cor_mpg_wt <- cor(mtcars$mpg, mtcars$wt)      expect_true(cor_mpg_wt >= -1 & cor_mpg_wt <= 1)   expect_true(cor_mpg_wt < 0)  # Should be negative correlation   expect_true(abs(cor_mpg_wt) > 0.5)  # Should be strong correlation })  test_that("linear model fitting works", {   data(mtcars)      # Fit simple linear model   model <- lm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars)      # Test model object   expect_s3_class(model, "lm")   expect_equal(length(coef(model)), 3)  # Intercept + 2 predictors   expect_true(summary(model)$r.squared > 0.5)   expect_true(summary(model)$r.squared <= 1)      # Test residuals   residuals <- resid(model)   expect_equal(length(residuals), nrow(mtcars))   expect_true(abs(mean(residuals)) < 1e-10)  # Should be approximately zero })

code: # tests/testthat/test-penguins-viz.R library(testthat) library(ggplot2) library(palmerpenguins)  # Helper function create_penguin_scatterplot <- function(data, x_var, y_var) {   ggplot(data, aes_string(x = x_var, y = y_var, color = "species")) +     geom_point() +     theme_minimal() +     labs(title = paste("Penguins:", y_var, "vs", x_var)) }  test_that("penguin scatterplot creation works", {   data(penguins, package = "palmerpenguins")      # Remove missing values for plotting   clean_penguins <- penguins[complete.cases(penguins), ]   expect_true(nrow(clean_penguins) > 0)      # Create plot   p <- create_penguin_scatterplot(clean_penguins, "bill_length_mm",                                   "body_mass_g")      # Test plot object   expect_s3_class(p, "ggplot")   expect_equal(length(p$layers), 1)  # One geom_point layer      # Test plot data   plot_data <- ggplot_build(p)$data[[1]]   expect_equal(nrow(plot_data), nrow(clean_penguins))   expect_true(all(c("x", "y", "colour") %in% names(plot_data))) })  test_that("penguin data visualization validates species separation", {   data(penguins, package = "palmerpenguins")   clean_penguins <- penguins[complete.cases(penguins), ]      # Create species summary for visualization validation   species_summary <- aggregate(body_mass_g ~ species,                                data = clean_penguins,                                FUN = function(x) c(mean = mean(x),                                                   sd = sd(x)))      expect_equal(nrow(species_summary), 3)  # Three species   expect_true(all(species_summary$body_mass_g[, "mean"] > 0))   expect_true(all(species_summary$body_mass_g[, "sd"] > 0))      # Gentoo penguins should be heaviest on average   gentoo_mean <- species_summary[species_summary$species == "Gentoo",                                  "body_mass_g"][1, "mean"]   adelie_mean <- species_summary[species_summary$species == "Adelie",                                 "body_mass_g"][1, "mean"]   expect_true(gentoo_mean > adelie_mean) })

code: # tests/testthat/test-iris-pipeline.R library(testthat)  # Analysis pipeline functions prepare_iris_data <- function(data) {   # Add derived variables   data$sepal_ratio <- data$Sepal.Length / data$Sepal.Width   data$petal_ratio <- data$Petal.Length / data$Petal.Width   data$total_length <- data$Sepal.Length + data$Petal.Length   return(data) }  analyze_species_differences <- function(data) {   # Perform ANOVA for each measurement   results <- list()   measurements <- c("Sepal.Length", "Sepal.Width", "Petal.Length",                     "Petal.Width")      for (measure in measurements) {     formula_str <- paste(measure, "~ Species")     aov_result <- aov(as.formula(formula_str), data = data)     results[[measure]] <- summary(aov_result)   }      return(results) }  test_that("iris data preparation pipeline works", {   data(iris)      # Test data preparation   prepared_data <- prepare_iris_data(iris)      # Check new variables were added   expect_true("sepal_ratio" %in% names(prepared_data))   expect_true("petal_ratio" %in% names(prepared_data))   expect_true("total_length" %in% names(prepared_data))      # Check derived variables are reasonable   expect_true(all(prepared_data$sepal_ratio > 0))   expect_true(all(prepared_data$petal_ratio > 0))   expect_true(all(prepared_data$total_length >                    prepared_data$Sepal.Length))   expect_true(all(prepared_data$total_length >                    prepared_data$Petal.Length))      # Check no missing values introduced   expect_false(any(is.na(prepared_data$sepal_ratio)))   expect_false(any(is.na(prepared_data$petal_ratio))) })  test_that("species analysis produces valid results", {   data(iris)   prepared_data <- prepare_iris_data(iris)      # Run analysis   analysis_results <- analyze_species_differences(prepared_data)      # Check structure of results   expect_true(is.list(analysis_results))   expect_equal(length(analysis_results), 4)   expect_true(all(c("Sepal.Length", "Sepal.Width", "Petal.Length",                      "Petal.Width") %in% names(analysis_results)))      # Check each ANOVA result   for (result in analysis_results) {     expect_true(is.list(result))     expect_true(length(result) == 1)  # One ANOVA table          # Check F-statistic exists and is positive     f_stat <- result[[1]][1, "F value"]     expect_true(f_stat > 0)          # Check p-value is between 0 and 1     p_value <- result[[1]][1, "Pr(>F)"]     expect_true(p_value >= 0 & p_value <= 1)   } })  test_that("complete iris analysis pipeline is reproducible", {   data(iris)      # Run pipeline twice   set.seed(123)   prepared1 <- prepare_iris_data(iris)   results1 <- analyze_species_differences(prepared1)      set.seed(123)   prepared2 <- prepare_iris_data(iris)   results2 <- analyze_species_differences(prepared2)      # Results should be identical   expect_equal(prepared1, prepared2)   expect_equal(results1, results2) })

code: # tests/testthat/test-mtcars-advanced.R library(testthat)  # Advanced analysis functions perform_regression_analysis <- function(data, response, predictors) {   formula_str <- paste(response, "~", paste(predictors, collapse = " + "))   model <- lm(as.formula(formula_str), data = data)      # Add model diagnostics   list(     model = model,     summary = summary(model),     diagnostics = list(       residuals = resid(model),       fitted = fitted(model),       r_squared = summary(model)$r.squared,       adj_r_squared = summary(model)$adj.r.squared,       aic = AIC(model),       bic = BIC(model)     )   ) }  validate_model_assumptions <- function(model_result) {   residuals <- model_result$diagnostics$residuals   fitted <- model_result$diagnostics$fitted      # Shapiro-Wilk test for normality (if sample size allows)   if (length(residuals) <= 5000) {     normality_test <- shapiro.test(residuals)   } else {     normality_test <- NULL   }      # Durbin-Watson test for autocorrelation   if (requireNamespace("lmtest", quietly = TRUE)) {     dw_test <- lmtest::dwtest(model_result$model)   } else {     dw_test <- NULL   }      list(     normality = normality_test,     durbin_watson = dw_test,     residual_range = range(residuals),     mean_residual = mean(residuals)   ) }  test_that("regression analysis function works correctly", {   data(mtcars)      # Test basic regression   result <- perform_regression_analysis(mtcars, "mpg",                                         c("wt", "hp", "cyl"))      # Check structure   expect_true(is.list(result))   expect_true(all(c("model", "summary", "diagnostics") %in% names(result)))      # Check model object   expect_s3_class(result$model, "lm")   expect_equal(length(coef(result$model)), 4)  # Intercept + 3 predictors      # Check diagnostics   expect_true(result$diagnostics$r_squared >= 0 &                result$diagnostics$r_squared <= 1)   expect_true(result$diagnostics$adj_r_squared <=                result$diagnostics$r_squared)   expect_true(result$diagnostics$aic > 0)   expect_true(result$diagnostics$bic > 0)   expect_equal(length(result$diagnostics$residuals), nrow(mtcars)) })  test_that("model validation catches assumption violations", {   data(mtcars)      # Create a good model   good_result <- perform_regression_analysis(mtcars, "mpg", c("wt", "hp"))   good_validation <- validate_model_assumptions(good_result)      # Check that validation runs without error   expect_true(is.list(good_validation))   expect_true(abs(good_validation$mean_residual) < 1e-10)  # Near zero      # Test with a poor model (if we had one)   # This tests that our validation function works   expect_true(length(good_validation$residual_range) == 2)   expect_true(good_validation$residual_range[1] <=                good_validation$residual_range[2]) })  test_that("regression handles different model specifications", {   data(mtcars)      # Test different model complexities   simple_model <- perform_regression_analysis(mtcars, "mpg", "wt")   complex_model <- perform_regression_analysis(mtcars, "mpg",                                               c("wt", "hp", "cyl",                                                 "disp", "qsec"))      # Simple model should have fewer parameters   expect_true(length(coef(simple_model$model)) <                length(coef(complex_model$model)))      # Complex model might have higher R-squared but not necessarily better AIC   expect_true(complex_model$diagnostics$r_squared >=                simple_model$diagnostics$r_squared)      # Both should be valid model objects   expect_s3_class(simple_model$model, "lm")   expect_s3_class(complex_model$model, "lm") })

code: # tests/testthat/test-penguins-integration.R library(testthat) library(palmerpenguins) library(ggplot2)  # Complete analysis workflow run_penguin_analysis <- function(remove_na = TRUE) {   # Load and prepare data   data(penguins, package = "palmerpenguins")      if (remove_na) {     clean_data <- penguins[complete.cases(penguins), ]   } else {     clean_data <- penguins   }      # Descriptive statistics   summary_stats <- list()   numeric_vars <- c("bill_length_mm", "bill_depth_mm", "flipper_length_mm",                     "body_mass_g")      for (var in numeric_vars) {     if (remove_na) {       values <- clean_data[[var]]     } else {       values <- clean_data[[var]][!is.na(clean_data[[var]])]     }          summary_stats[[var]] <- list(       mean = mean(values),       sd = sd(values),       median = median(values),       range = range(values)     )   }      # Statistical models   models <- list()      # Model 1: Body mass predicted by bill dimensions   if (remove_na) {     models$body_mass_model <- lm(body_mass_g ~ bill_length_mm +                                  bill_depth_mm, data = clean_data)   }      # Model 2: Flipper length by species   if (remove_na) {     models$flipper_species_model <- aov(flipper_length_mm ~ species,                                         data = clean_data)   }      # Visualizations   plots <- list()      if (remove_na && nrow(clean_data) > 0) {     # Scatterplot     plots$bill_scatter <- ggplot(clean_data,                                  aes(x = bill_length_mm,                                      y = bill_depth_mm,                                      color = species)) +       geom_point() +       theme_minimal() +       labs(title = "Bill Dimensions by Species")          # Box plot     plots$mass_boxplot <- ggplot(clean_data,                                  aes(x = species, y = body_mass_g,                                      fill = species)) +       geom_boxplot() +       theme_minimal() +       labs(title = "Body Mass by Species")   }      # Return comprehensive results   list(     data = clean_data,     summary_stats = summary_stats,     models = models,     plots = plots,     sample_size = nrow(clean_data)   ) }  test_that("complete penguin analysis workflow executes successfully", {   # Run complete analysis   expect_no_error({     results <- run_penguin_analysis(remove_na = TRUE)   })      # Check overall structure   expect_true(is.list(results))   expect_true(all(c("data", "summary_stats", "models", "plots",                      "sample_size") %in% names(results)))      # Check data   expect_s3_class(results$data, "data.frame")   expect_true(results$sample_size > 300)  # Should have substantial sample   expect_false(any(is.na(results$data)))  # No missing values after cleaning      # Check summary statistics   expect_equal(length(results$summary_stats), 4)   for (stat in results$summary_stats) {     expect_true(all(c("mean", "sd", "median", "range") %in% names(stat)))     expect_true(stat$mean > 0)     expect_true(stat$sd > 0)   }      # Check models   expect_s3_class(results$models$body_mass_model, "lm")   expect_s3_class(results$models$flipper_species_model, "aov")      # Check plots   expect_s3_class(results$plots$bill_scatter, "ggplot")   expect_s3_class(results$plots$mass_boxplot, "ggplot") })  test_that("penguin analysis produces biologically meaningful results", {   results <- run_penguin_analysis(remove_na = TRUE)      # Check that Gentoo penguins are heaviest (known biological fact)   species_masses <- aggregate(body_mass_g ~ species, data = results$data,                               mean)   gentoo_mass <- species_masses[species_masses$species == "Gentoo",                                 "body_mass_g"]   adelie_mass <- species_masses[species_masses$species == "Adelie",                                 "body_mass_g"]   chinstrap_mass <- species_masses[species_masses$species == "Chinstrap",                                    "body_mass_g"]      expect_true(gentoo_mass > adelie_mass)   expect_true(gentoo_mass > chinstrap_mass)      # Check that body mass model is significant   model_summary <- summary(results$models$body_mass_model)   expect_true(model_summary$r.squared > 0.1)  # At least some explanatory                                               # power   expect_true(model_summary$fstatistic[1] > 1)  # F-statistic should be > 1      # Check that species differences in flipper length are significant   anova_result <- summary(results$models$flipper_species_model)   f_stat <- anova_result[[1]][1, "F value"]   p_value <- anova_result[[1]][1, "Pr(>F)"]   expect_true(f_stat > 1)   expect_true(p_value < 0.05)  # Should be significant })  test_that("penguin analysis is reproducible with different options", {   # Test reproducibility with NA removal   results1 <- run_penguin_analysis(remove_na = TRUE)   results2 <- run_penguin_analysis(remove_na = TRUE)      # Data should be identical   expect_equal(results1$data, results2$data)   expect_equal(results1$sample_size, results2$sample_size)      # Summary statistics should be identical   expect_equal(results1$summary_stats, results2$summary_stats)      # Model coefficients should be identical   expect_equal(coef(results1$models$body_mass_model),                 coef(results2$models$body_mass_model)) })
